EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm over the north Pacific generated large swell which produced high surf ranging from 10 to 20 feet.  Heavy beach erosion occurred along the north shore of Oahu.  Many county and state beach parks were closed along the Kona Coast of the Island of Hawaii and the road pavement was washed away from the road surface of Alii Drive in Kailua Kona.

An intense storm over the north Pacific generated large swell which produced high surf ranging from 10 to 20 feet.  Heavy beach erosion occurred along the north shore of Oahu.  Many county and state beach parks were closed along the Kona Coast of the Island of Hawaii and the road pavement was washed away from the road surface of Alii Drive in Kailua Kona.
Funnel cloud observed above village of Volcano.

A mesoscale low was detected by the Kauai WSR-88D during the early evening of the 24th just north of Oahu.  Velocity and Storm Relative Velocity products indicated cyclonic circulation from 15 to 20 thousand feet.  After 0100 HST on the 25th, the low began a slow drift south over Oahu.  Heavy rains occurred over windward Oahu as the low moved over the island.  Rainfall totals (inches) included: 9.02 at Olomana, 9.00 at Kailua, 7.78 at Maunawili, 6.15 at Kaneohe, 4.48 at Punaluu, and 5.00 at Kahuku.  60 Waimanalo residents were evacuated as flooding and mudslides closed many windward Oahu roads.  15 homes were damaged on Kakaina and Mekia Streets in Waimanalo.  A bridge over the Makawao Stream was washed out, isolating 30 residents near the top of Maunawili Valley.  Mudslides closed the Pali Highway, a major traffic artery between windward Oahu and Honolulu.  High water closed the Kapaa Quarry Road, Kalanianaole Highway in Waimanalo, and the Kahekili Highway.
Heavy snow accumulating to 8 inches, with winds to 50 mph causing considerable drifting snow of 4 to 6 feet closed the road from 10,000 ft to the 13,796 ft summit of Mauna Kea.

A fall storm in the southern hemisphere, to the south of Tahiti, generated large ocean swell around May 19th.  Surf with sets to 6 feet built along the south shores of the Hawaiian Islands during the late morning hours of the 26th.  Lifeguards conducted 100 water rescues by waverunner, including 33 at Waikiki.  Three surfers were injured.  A rouge wave estimated at 15 to 20 feet capsized a boat with a failed engine in Keehi Lagoon.  The boat broke up on a nearby reef.  One man drowned and three others were rescued.
Lightning struck the Maui Space Surveillance Complex at the summit of Haleakala sparking an electrical fire in a power distribution panel.  Three people were treated for smoke inhalation.
Lightning that struck electric lines caused power outages in the Kau and lower Puna Districts.
Two warehouse roofs were damaged at Pearl Harbor.
Funnel cloud southeast of Hilo Airport.

Severe thunderstorm winds blew down numerous trees.  An employee at the Pacific Missile Range Facility at Barking Sands on the west coast of Kauai reported the heaviest squall line he had seen in 17 years of observing weather.  Winds at Kokee were estimated at 60 mph.

Flash flooding briefly closed the Kohala Mountain Road.  North Kohala received 2.8 inches of rain, easing the ongoing drought.  Nearly 1.5 inches of rain fell at Kealakekua and Honaunau in the South Kona District.
Funnel cloud south of Diamond Head observed by weather spotter.
Funnel cloud over southeast Molokai.
Three waterspouts observed north of Molokai.
Waterspout stationary on Kaneohe Bay.

Strong south and southwest winds ahead of low pressure and a cold front approaching the Hawaiian Islands produced winds to 90 mph over the summits and upper slopes of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.  High winds also buffeted Waimea and Kamuela where winds accelerated between the Kohala Mountains and Mauna Kea.

Strong south and southwest winds ahead of low pressure and a cold front approaching the Hawaiian Islands produced winds to 90 mph over the summits and upper slopes of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.  High winds also buffeted Waimea and Kamuela where winds accelerated between the Kohala Mountains and Mauna Kea.
Wind gusts to 45 mph were reported at Barbers Point, and by weather spotters in Honolulu, Kailua, and Kahuluu.  Trees downed on highway near Kuoloa.

Mid level winds in the south quadrant of a low centered about 600 miles north of Hawaii brought 40 to 60 mph winds to the summits and upper slopes of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.  The accompanying wind chill was 5 below zero to 20 below zero.
West to southwest winds of 60 mph with higher gusts were recorded at the United Kingdom Observatory on Mauna Kea.
A waterspout was observed off of the north shore of Oahu.

Heavy showers associated with a southeast moving cold front flooded parts of state highway 190 between the Waimea Kohala Airport and the Saddle Road turnoff.  Heaviest showers were in the Waimea area. Minor road flooding also occurred along the Hamakua Coast from Honokaa to near Laupahoehoe.
Southwest winds of 40 to 60 mph affected the summits and upper slopes of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa.

An upper level low over Oahu destabilized the area and heavy showers occurred over the central and north parts of the Koolau Mountains.  Localized flooding of streams and streets was reported in the Waimea Bay and Sacred Falls areas.  In Honolulu, Kalihi Street was closed near Kalaepaa Drive due to high water.
Street flooding reported in the Lanai City area.
Streams flooded and water ponded on roads in areas of east Kauai.

Many roads had minor flooding in the following areas: Waialua, Haleiwa, Wahiawa, Mililani, Punaluu, Kaneohe, Kailua, Waimanalo, and Hawaii Kai.  Some small streams along the windward side of Oahualso produced minor flooding.
Road flooding reported in parts of the South Kohala District.

A 23-year-old National Guardsman was struck by lightning at Wheeler Army Air Field while standing behind a helicopter.  He was knocked unconscious for about 10 minutes and hospitalized for observation with only minor injury.  A mechanic working on a nearby helicopter saw a bright flash strike near the helicopter's rotor blade.

Heavy showers, ahead of a weak frontal boundary moving slowly southeast toward Oahu, brought small stream and street flooding to the Lualaulei area, the Waianae Coast from the town of Waianae northward, and central Oahu from Wahiawa northward.  The rain gage at Lualualei recorded 1.04 inches in 15 minutes.
A westward moving upper level low just north of Kauai set off heavy showers over the north shore.  Minor flooding of streams, drainage ditches and roads was reported in the Wainiha and Haena areas.

Heavy showers for a second day in a row produced small stream and street flooding in parts of the Waianae Range and Waianae Coast.  The Farrington Highway was closed due to high water in two locations and Homestead Road was flooded, blocking access to Lualualei Valley.  The rain gage at Lualaulei recorded 2.4 inches. During the 12th and 13th, Lualualei received nearly 5 inches of rain.

Localized heavy showers produced minor flooding, and runoff deposited debris on roads in the Kealakekua and Kailua-Kona areas.  Brief downpours with rainfall rates of 0.50 of an inch in 15 minutes were recorded.
Widespread street flooding was reported in the vicinity of Wai'aha Stream where 2.10 inches of rain fell in a 1 hour period.

Cooler air aloft with an upper low pressure center just north of the Hawaiian Islands kept the airmass unstable over all islands on the 9th.  Convection was focused over the islands during the afternoon hours.  Heavy showers lingered into the evening hours on Molokai where the runoff flooded some roads along the south side of the island.
Heavy showers produced runoff that washed out parts of the roadway along the south shore of Molokai between Kaunakakai and Kamalo.
A waterspout was observed over the Pailolo channel between Molokai and Lanai.
A waterspout was observed offshore of Koko Head.
A funnel cloud was observed in the vicinity of Kailua-Kona.

A waterspout was over the south end of Kaneohe Bay just offshore of Moku O Loe Island, also known as Coconut Island.  The waterspout was nearly stationary and was captured by still and video photographers.
A funnel cloud was observed over Pearl City.
Surf heights of 12 to 18 feet occurred along north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores.
Surf heights of 12 to 18 feet occurred along north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores.
Surf heights of 12 to 18 feet occurred along north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores.
Surf heights of 12 to 18 feet occurred along north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores.
Surf heights of 12 to 18 feet occurred along north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores.

Surf heights of 15 to 20 feet occurred along north facing shores and 10 to 12 feet along west facing shores.  All beach parks were closed along north and west facing shores of Oahu.  On Oahu's north shore, surf run up was reported into the yards of several homes and washed across coastal roads in the usual isolated locations.  Surface erosion occurred along Kawailoa, Pupukea, and Kenui Roads located close to the beach.

Southwest winds of 45 to 65 mph were recorded near the summit of Haleakala. Southwest winds of 60 to 85 mph were recorded near the summit of Mauna Kea.  At lower elevations of North Kohala, Kona, and Hilo Districts, winds gusting above 40 mph knocked several trees and power lines down.  2,000 customers lost power in North Kohala and 2,500 lost power in Hilo.
Surf heights of 8 to 12 feet occurred along north facing shores.
Surf heights of 8 to 12 feet occurred along north facing shores.

A storm low to the northeast of the state produced swell that impacted the north and northeast facing shores of all islands.  Surf heights were up to 12 feet on the 8th, 15 feet on the 9th, and occasionally as high as 20 feet on the 10th and 11th.  On Kauai, a rental home was swept off the foundation on the 11th.  On Oahu, surging waves spread rocks and debris along Kamehameha Highway from Kaaawa to Sunset Beach.  Bulldozers were used to clear debris from the highway in Punaluu, and the wave erosion caused the road to sink in some sections.  Harbor surges compounded the high water problems for Kahului and Hilo Harbors.  28 residents were evacuated from homes and encampments at Kings Landing, Keaukaha, and Radio Bay on the Big Island.  The beachfront at Kings Landing was destroyed.  High surf washed a Keaukaha home off its foundation and moved the structure 60 feet, blocking the access road to a nearby condominium complex.  Along Hilo Bay, there was damage inside the breakwater.  Rock sea walls fronting homes along Kumau Street broke down, the shoreline was heavily eroded, and debris was thrown into the yards of bay front homes.
Heavy showers produced minor flooding of roadways in the Hanapepe, Lihue, and Anahola areas.

Heavy showers produced widespread flooding of roads.  Rainfall included 3.26 inches at Kunia and 3.74 at Waipio in a 3 hour period.  Rainfall rates of 1 to 3 inches per hour occurred near Barbers Point, Pearl City, downtown Honolulu, Kailua, Maunawili, and Waimanalo.  Numerous roads were closed due to mudslides and high water throughout southwest, south central, east, and windward parts of Oahu.

Strong southwest winds several hundred miles in advance of an approaching cold front brought  frequent  gusts of 40 to 60 mph along the north and east portions of Oahu and Maui.  The winds accelerated as they moved down the steep slopes of the Koolau Mountains on Oahu, and the steep terrain of  west Maui and Haleakala.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest wind gusts estimated near 60 mph blew tree branches into power lines cutting electricity to about 2,000 residents in portions of the Haiku, Kula, Piiholo-Olinda, Mahinahina/Honokowai, Kahana, and Napili areas.
Minor road flooding and ponding was reported in the Kihei, Kula, Kahului, and central valley areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was spotted from Upolu Point over the Alenuihaha Channel.

An intense storm system to the northwest of the Kauai generated swell that impacted the north and west facing shores of all islands.  On the 14th, surf of 15 to 20 feet with occasional sets to 25 feet closed beaches on Oahu's north shore from Kahuku to Haleiwa, and from 10 to 15 feet on the west shores at Kaena Point State Park and Yokohama Bay.  Above normal tides caused additional high water during the early morning hours of the 14th with significant road damage to the Kamehameha Highway near Chun's Reef on the north shore.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed in the Kamuela, North and South Kohala, and Volcano village areas.
Power outages from Piiholo to Kula were caused by falling branches hitting electric lines.
Scattered power outages affected 1,600 homes when several trees and power poles fell in  Kailua, Kaneohe, and Waialua.  Eight homes had damaged roofs.
Westerly winds of 40 to 60 mph were recorded near the summit of Haleakala.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several lines of heavy showers and thunderstorms moved across Maui County from the southwest. On Maui, the Honoapiilani Highway was flooded and some mudslides occurred from McGregor Point to Napili.  The Piilani Highway was closed due to high water in spots from Milepost 36 to 37 between Kaupo and Kipahulu.  Minor flooding and ponding of roads was common across Maui.

Flooding occurred across the Kamehameha Highway in the vicinity of Kawela Stream, near mileposts 7 and 8, and near Kamalo.  Water was on the Maunawainui Bridge, and minor street flooding occurred in Hoolehua.
A line of heavy showers moved across Oahu, with 3 to 4 inch rainfall estimates from the WSR-88D in a 2 hour period.  Police reported minor street flooding in the Waianae and central Oahu.

An intense storm system to the northwest of the Kauai generated swell that impacted the north and west facing shores of all islands.  On the 14th, surf of 15 to 20 feet with occasional sets to 25 feet closed beaches on Oahu's north shore from Kahuku to Haleiwa, and from 10 to 15 feet on the west shores at Kaena Point State Park and Yokohama Bay.  Above normal tides caused additional high water during the early morning hours of the 14th with significant road damage to the Kamehameha Highway near Chun's Reef on the north shore.

Heavy rains over the already saturated parts of west, central, and north shore areas of Oahu produced widespread flooding.  On the Waianae Coast, the Makaha Valley Tower complex was hit by a massive mudslide at about 2 a.m., with extensive damage to the first floor of the complex and to many cars in the adjacent parking lot.  Major flooding occurred along Kamehameha Highway between Kahuku and Haleiwa.  Farrington Highway, Lagoon Drive, and the Nimitz Highway were closed in several spots due to high water.  Significant flooding also occurred in parts of windward Oahu and Honolulu where rainfall rates of nearly 1 inch in 15 minutes were recorded during the early morning hours, producing widespread ponding on roads that hampered commuters.  A Kaneohe resident was temporarily trapped in a sinkhole near Aumoana Place.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds with frequent gusts estimated at 60 mph damaged or destroyed the roofs of  36 homes in windward communities from Waimanalo to Kaaawa.  Snapped tree branches injured two people in Kaneohe and Kailua.  One person was injured by an airborne dumpster and another was  injured by a collapsing fence.  Scattered felled trees downed power lines disrupting power to approximately 12,500 homes in the Ahuimanu, Kaneohe, and Kailua areas.

Strong northerly winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts estimated as high as 60 mph accompanied the gradient associated with a large high pressure area to the northwest of the state and a deepening low pressure center several hundred miles to the northeast of Hawaii.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Scattered areas of felled trees knocked down power and telephone lines in Diamond Head, Hawaii Kai, Kaneohe, Maili, Makaha, Nanakuli, Palolo Valley, Waialae, Waianae, and Waikiki.  Some of the trees  were in areas of moist soil due to record setting November rains.  One person suffered minor injuries when hit by a small utility pole near Kapiolani Blvd and Harding Av in Honolulu.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surf heights of 15 to 20 feet occurred along north facing shores and 10 to 12 feet along west facing shores.  All beach parks were closed along north and west facing shores of Oahu.  On Oahu's north shore, surf run up was reported into the yards of several homes and washed across coastal roads in the usual isolated locations.  Surface erosion occurred along Kawailoa, Pupukea, and Kenui Roads located close to the beach.
Surf heights of 8 to 12 feet occurred along north facing shores.
Surf heights of 8 to 12 feet occurred along north facing shores.
Westerly winds of  35 to 50 mph were recorded at the summit of Haleakala.  Westerly winds of  50 to 70 mph with highest gusts to 97 mph were recorded near the summit of Mauna Kea.

A cold front moving from northwest to southeast brought strong southerly winds to all of the main Hawaiian Islands during December 26-27th.  Winds ranged from 35 to 45 mph with gusts estimated as high as 75  mph where the winds accelerated down the north facing slopes of the mountains on the islands of  Kauai, Oahu, Maui, and Hawaii.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding was reported in west Maui, the central valley, and east Maui, including Makawao, Haiku, and Kailua.  Minor mud and rock slides were along parts of Honoapiilani Highway and near Maalaea Harbor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough located north of the Hawaiian Islands with a jetstream wind maximum of 150 knots persisted along 30N latitude for over 4 days.   High winds along the south edge of the jetstream buffeted the higher volcano summits of Hawaii and Maui.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds of 60 to 80 mph were recorded near the summit of Mauna Kea.

Surf heights of 10 to 15 feet occurred along the north facing shores and 6 to 10 feet along west facing shores.

Surf heights of 8 to 12 feet occurred along north facing shores.
Heavy showers caused ponding on roads and small streams to flood from Lihue to Anahola.  The showers were associated with the Big Island plume and moved into the area from the southeast.

Strong northerly winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts estimated as high as 60 mph accompanied the gradient associated with a large high pressure area to the northwest of the state and a deepening low pressure center several hundred miles to the northeast of Hawaii.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and branches knocked down a few power lines in the South Kohala and North and South Kona Districts. Two power poles snapped and sparked a brush fire that burned 1 acre of state land in Kona Palisades.  A pole was also downed at Spencer Beach Park, causing power outages at several hotels.

At lower elevations of North Kohala, Kona, and Hilo Districts, winds gusting above 40 mph knocked several trees and power lines down.  2,000 customers lost power in North Kohala and 2,500 lost power in Hilo.
Heavy showers produced minor flooding of roads in the Hanapepe area.

Above normal tides caused additional high water during the early morning hours of the 14th with significant road damage to the Kamehameha Highway near Chun's Reef on the north shore.
Showers produced heavy rainfall over the west, central, and Honolulu portions of Oahu, with minor flooding of roads throughout Waianae, Ewa Beach, Waipahu, and sections of Honolulu.

A drought emergency was declared for Upcountry Maui, and the North Kohala, South Kohala, and Hamakua Districts of Hawaii, where an order for a mandatory 25% reduction in water use was in effect by the end of the month.  For Upcountry Maui, the order was the most stringent since 1984, and more than 8,000 residents, businesses and farmers in the areas of Haiku, Makawao, Haliimaile, Olinda, Pukalani, Omaopio-Pulehu, Kula, Keokea, Ulupalakua, and Kanaio were affected.  Stabilizing high pressure over the area diminished the usual moisture laden trade wind pattern.  Only a trace of rain fell in Kahului which usually receives 1.23 inches.  0.42 inches fell in Waimea where the normal for the month is 3.3 inches.  Total rainfall since July was about one-third of normal.   Sugar, onion, and pineapple crops were reduced on Maui, while lettuce and cabbage suffered on Hawaii.  On Hawaii ranchlands, a 10% decrease in cow births was attributed to the drought.

Showers producing heavy rainfall caused minor flooding of roads and streams throughout Molokai.
Heavy rains of 2.5 to 4 inches in a 2 hour period caused flooding along the Hanalei River.  Rising waters prompted the evacuation of residents from the east side of the Hanapepe River.
Thunderstorms with heavy showers flooded roads from Waianae to Nanakuli where the Farrington Highway was temporarily closed due to high water.
A funnel cloud associated with a northeast to southwest oriented line of thunderstorms just offshore from Poipu was reported by a Skywarn weather spotter.
A Waterspout was reported 6 miles offshore.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding of roads due to clogged storm drains and flooding in low-lying areas such as Kihei.  Ponding and debris on the roads in Lanai City, and minor stream flooding on Molokai.
Ponding on roads reported over the east part of Kauai.  Wainiha Bridge limited to one lane due to ponding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system to the northwest of the Kauai generated swell that impacted the north and west facing shores of all islands.  On the 14th, surf of 15 to 20 feet with occasional sets to 25 feet closed beaches on Oahu's north shore from Kahuku to Haleiwa, and from 10 to 15 feet on the west shores at Kaena Point State Park and Yokohama Bay.  Above normal tides caused additional high water during the early morning hours of the 14th with significant road damage to the Kamehameha Highway near Chun's Reef on the north shore.

Record breaking rainfall produced widespread flooding with associated mud and rock slides, road closures, blocked drainage systems, and overwhelmed waste disposal systems.  Damage to public and private property and agriculture was substantial.  The Waianae Coast was hardest hit with people stranded in their homes for up to a week in some areas with persistent standing water due to the saturated soils.At the beginning of the month, a high amplitude pattern developed with a strong upper level trough over the central Pacific.  From the 3rd through 5th, the upper level trough and surface low pressure were located west northwest of Kauai.  On the 5th, heavy showers and thunderstorms moved north in convergent low level flow, and produced 12 inches of rain in less than 12 hours over parts of southwest Oahu.  On the 7th and 8th, the surface low pressure deepened rapidly and moved north, producing large swell and damaging surf from the 8th through 11th along the north facing shores of all islands.The upper level trough was nearly stationary as the first surface low center moved to the northeast and away from the islands.  A second surface low pressure then developed and strengthened west of Kauai, while the upper level trough shifted east and centered over Oahu.  From the 12th through 16th, the upper level trough persisted in the vicinity of Oahu.  Weak disturbances rotated around the base of the trough bringing more heavy rains to Oahu.  Although the highest rain totals occurred on Oahu, rainfall was widespread from Kauai to Molokai during the period.Rainfall totals (in inches) for some locations on Oahu through the first 15 days of the month and their averages for the entire month:                First 15 days     Average for monthPoamoho         22.4              4.6Palisades       21.4              8.0Waianae         21.3              2.0Kunia           19.2              3.3Waipio          19.2              3.3Lualualei       18.8              2.5Mililani        18.5              4.5Wilson Tunnel   17.8             10.2Waihee Pump     17.2             16.2Hon Intl Arpt   16.1              3.0Aloha Tower     14.9              2.6Maunawili       14.3              7.6Kahuku          11.5              4.6At Honolulu International Airport, rainfall was 18.74 inches for the entire month, establishing a new record for the month of November.  The previous record for November was 14.72 inches.  The monthly total of 18.74 inches exceeded the total for all of 1995, which was 13.60 inches.  One of the highest rainfall reports came from a NWS cooperative observer in Waipahu, where 31.47 inches of rain was recorded for the month.

Heavy rainfall caused widespread flooding in Ewa, Honolai Hale, and the Pearl City/Aiea areas.  Maximum WSR-88D rainfall estimates were 12.5 inches over ungaged areas of the Ewa Plain.  Kunia received 3.74 inches in 2 hours, a rate statistically expected only once in 100 years.  People were evacuated from their homes along Renton and Puuloa Roads.  Rain destroyed some small farms in the Pearlridge area.  Runoff from the Waianae Mountains near Honolai Hale closed the H1 Freeway in both directions.  The flood carried mud, rocks, and debris onto the highway, cutting the Leeward Coast off from the rest of Oahu for several hours.  Rainfall totals (in inches) for the period 1000 to 1700 HST include: Ewa Beach 12.10, Kunia Substation 8.50, Palisades 8.00, Waiwa 7.15, Waipio 5.87, and Mililani 5.54.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers produced minor street flooding from Mana to Kekaha to Waimea and in the Lihue area.
Scattered areas of minor street flooding reported across Oahu.
Several small waterspouts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surf heights of 8 to 12 feet occurred along north facing shores .

Surf heights of 15 to 20 feet occurred along north facing shores and 10 to 12 feet along west facing shores.  All beach parks were closed along north and west facing shores of Oahu.  On Oahu's north shore, surf run up was reported into the yards of several homes and washed across coastal roads in the usual isolated locations.  Surface erosion occurred along Kawailoa, Pupukea, and Kenui Roads located close to the beach.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor stream and road flooding due to heavy showers throughout the island of Molokai.

Heavy rain produced flooding which closed and severely eroded the road between Kaupo and Kipahulu.  The Wainee plantation village was also flooded.  Small landslides partially blocked the lanes  of the Honoapiilani Highway on the Kahakuloa side of Honolua Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds of  35 to 50 mph were recorded at the summit of Haleakala.  Westerly winds of  50 to 70 mph with highest gusts to 97 mph were recorded near the summit of Mauna Kea.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Molokai, small streams overflowed and the coast road had minor flooding in several locations  east of Kaluaaha.  Minor street flooding ocurred on Maui and Lanai.
Minor flooding of streets and low-lying areas reported in the following districts: North and South Kohala,  North and South Kona, Hilo, and Puna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense storm system to the northwest of the Kauai generated swell that impacted the north and west facing shores of all islands.  On the 14th, surf of 15 to 20 feet with occasional sets to 25 feet closed beaches on Oahu's north shore from Kahuku to Haleiwa, and from 10 to 15 feet on the west shores at Kaena Point State Park and Yokohama Bay.  Above normal tides caused additional high water during the early morning hours of the 14th with significant road damage to the Kamehameha Highway near Chun's Reef on the north shore.
Heavy runoff from showers over the mountain slopes of east Maui caused overflowing streams from Hana to Kaupo.

Heavy showers produced street flooding in Manoa, Nuuanu and Moiliili.  Although there were no reports of streams exceeding their banks, a 50 year-old woman was swept away and drowned when she fell into the rain swollen Waiomao Stream above the Palolo Valley.  She lost her balance and fell into the stream while rescuing a boy who had fallen into the stream during a day hike.

Scattered heavy showers persisted over windward and mountain parts of the islands of Hawaii, Maui, Molokai, and Oahu from late in the day on the 30th through the early morning hours of the 31st.  The heaviest rains occurred on Maui where the raingage at Mahinahina recorded 5.29 inches in a 12 hour period.  Flooding occurred near several bridges along the road to Hana, and minor road flooding was reported in West Maui and on the other islands.

Funnel cloud reported over Fern Street at Kalakaua Avenue by a Skywarn spotter.  Winds were estimated at 50 mph in the vicinity of the funnel and ripped roofing paper from one house, and tore several pieces of fiberglass roofing material from a nearby business.

Southwest winds of 50 to 65 mph occurred over the summits and upper slopes of Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa for about 48 hours, with a top wind speed of 69 mph recorded at the United Kingdom Observatory on the morning of the 27th.

Large ocean swell generated by a distant north Pacific storm brought high surf to the Hawaiian Islands. The northwest swell produced high surf of up to 15 feet along northwest facing shores.On the Big Island, a 48 year old man was killed shortly after 0900 HST when the 25-foot fishing boat he was in capsized just outside the entrance to Honokohau Harbor near Kailua-Kona.
Westerly winds of 60 mph were recorded at the United Kingdom observation site atop Mauna Kea at 2000 HST on the 21st.

A stationary storm over the northeast Pacific centered 1,200 miles north-northeast of Hawaii, generated large north swells that caused high surf and surges for Hilo Harbor.  The U.S.C.G. Cutter Kiska sustained $100,000 in damage while moored in the Radio Bay area of Hilo Harbor.  According to the commanding officer, extreme surging action developed as large waves filled the mooring area.  Wave heights of 15 to 20 feet occurred outside Hilo Harbor and 6 to 10 feet inside the harbor.  Ocean front streets were closed in Hilo and several lower units of a condominium were flooded in Keaukaha, 3 miles southeast of Hilo.  Cars were damaged in the flooded parking lots of two condominium buildings.

A surface low pressure center formed near Johnston Island on the morning of March 2nd.  Strong southerly flow to the east of the low center pulled large amounts of moisture northward from the intertropical convergence zone.  The deep tropical moisture interacted with an old frontal boundary extending westward from near South Point.  Rain amounts were uniform for most of the islands, ranging from 2 to 5 inches in the 48 hour period from 8 am on the 2nd through 8 am on the 4th.  The exception was the Big Island where strong low level southeast flow focused on the east side of the island and helicity values were high.  8 to 10 inch rains fell over parts of Kau, Puna, Hilo, and Hamakua during the same 48 hour period, and rainfall rates of 3 inches per hour between 2 am and 4 am on the 3rd caused flash flooding.  The flooding caused spot evacuations from Hamakua to Puna.  Water 6 feet deep closed Highway 11 near Pahala, and the Kau District was divided by water runoff at Kawa Flats, blocking traffic between Pahala and Naalehu.  Landslides closed the belt highway for nearly 5 hours at Laupahoehoe and Maulua gulches on the 3rd.  Rainfall amounts for the 24 hours ending at 8 pm, on the 3rd included:  Pahala, 9.65 inches; Pahoa, 9 inches; Laupahoehoe, 8.29 inches; and Hilo 6.70 inches. As the low center deepened and moved northeast, strong southerly winds developed over the islands.  The Molokai WSR-88D indicated 40 to 50 knot winds at low levels during the morning of March 3rd.Significant wind damage occurred on the Big Island with winds of 40 to 70 mph in many downslope and terrain funneled locations.  Damage to papaya crops and orchids, and agricultural facilities was extensive on the Big Island.  Three dozen homes had wind damage on the Big Island, some with roofs blown off and others damaged by downed trees.  People were evacuated from the Volcano House where 60 mph winds blew off part of the hotel roof.  One guest cut his right arm when hit by a piece of flying debris from the hotel roof.On Maui, power was cut to over 40% of the island when power poles were downed by 50 to 60 mph winds.  6 large power poles were downed along the Hana Highway blocking traffic from Upcountry and East Maui areas headed to the west.  All TV and radio transmitters atop Haleakala suffered wind damage by 60 to 80 mph winds, cutting service for parts of the 3rd and 4th.  Three boats ere pulled from moorings.  A 20 foot sailboat sank in Ma'alaea Bay, while 2 other vessels were grounded.  All public schools were closed on the 4th.  The roof of one home was damaged in Waihee.  Wind reports also include: 58 mph gusts at Pukalani (1200 ft) at 1:06 pm on the 3rd, and 53 mph at WSO Kahului at 3:30 am on the 4th.On Oahu, scattered wind damage occurred.  Kaneohe reported wind gusts to 61 mph and a roof was blown from a home.  In Kailua, a roof was blown off a carport, and in Ewa Beach a roof was blown off a home.  In Waimanalo, sudden wind gusts damaged three buildings on Huli Street behind Kaaiai Street shortly after 1 am on the 3rd, including a garage roof, a patio roof, and a home roof.  Fallen poles and trees were reported in scattered areas.  Saturated ground caused three utility poles to lean over Kunia Road, makai of Waipahu Street, about 1:40 pm on the 3rd.  5,000 homes were without power for several hours.  Kunia road was closed between the Hawaii Country Club and Anonui Road when 13 utility poles were blown down causing 14,000 homes to lose power.In Honolulu, a large Kiawi tree fell and blocked Punahou Street between Wilder Avenue and H-1 during the early morning hours of the 4th.  Windows were broken by wind gusts at a small office at Ala Moana Shopping Center, and also at a home on Kapiolani Boulevard.

A storm low far north of the Hawaiian Islands generated swell that impacted the north and west facing shores producing surf of 18 to 25 feet in the Waimea and Sunset Beach areas of Oahu.  Surf run up washed across some north shore roads, and most beaches were closed due to the life threatening surf.

Surf run up washed across some north shore roads, and most beaches were closed due to the life threatening surf.

A strong mid level jetstream associated with a cold front brought wind gusts of 125 mph to Mauna Kea and Mauna Loa, and to 105 mph at the summit of Haleakala.  The highest winds occurred early on the 11th.  At lower elevations, gusts to 45 mph toppled about 40 trees in Hilo and a dozen in the Puna District.  One home was damaged by a large tree in Hilo.

Winds of 68 mph were reported by the observatories on Mauna Kea.
Heavy rains caused widespread flooding of the Hanalei Valley and the closing of the Hanalei River Bridge.  9 inches of rain fell at       Hanalei in a 12 hour period.

Heavy rains over central Oahu and north Oahu from Haleiwa to Kahuku caused minor street flooding.  A weather spotter in Kahuku reported 1.2 inches of rain in a 30 minute period during the late afternoon hours.

Winds of 60 mph with gusts to 100 mph were recorded at the summit of Mauna Kea. At sea level, prolonged gales over adjacent coastal waters and channels kept barges with food supplies from leaving their home ports.  Supplies of some food staples were critically low on Maui, Molokai, Lanai, and west Hawaii.

Heavy showers along the north slopes of Haleakala and the Hana Road caused heavy runoff.  The Haiku Road was closed due to high water.  A weather spotter in Haiku reported 7.7 inches of rain in 10 hours.
Heavy showers and thunderstorms over the north and south Kona Districts caused widespread flooding of roads and low-lying areas.

A storm low in the southern hemisphere, centered south of Tahiti on the 16th and 17th, produced large swell that arrived along the south shores of the Hawaiian Islands late in the day on the 22nd.  Surf of 6 to 8 feet was reported along south shores of Oahu, and 8 to 12 feet along south shores of Kauai from the 22nd through the 24th.  At 0930 HST on the 23rd, an 18 ft boat capsized at the entrance to Waianae Boat Harbor.  Two people were rescued.  One man was treated for a knee injury.  A total of 95 people were rescued from the high surf along the south shore of Oahu by water safety personnel during the 22nd and 23rd.  On Maui, Kamaole Beach Park in Kihei was closed.

Ocean swell generated by a storm low far to the north of the Hawaiian Islands produced surf of 10 to 15 feet along the north and west facing shores.  A man was swept into the ocean and drowned by the high surf while meditating on a ledge just north of Haiku, Maui near Pauwela Point Lighthouse at approximately 0715 HST on the 29th.

A drought emergency was declared for Upcountry Maui, and the North Kohala, South Kohala, and Hamakua Districts of Hawaii, where an order for a mandatory 25% reduction in water use was in effect by the end of the month.  For Upcountry Maui, the order was the most stringent since 1984, and more than 8,000 residents, businesses and farmers in the areas of Haiku, Makawao, Haliimaile, Olinda, Pukalani, Omaopio-Pulehu, Kula, Keokea, Ulupalakua, and Kanaio were affected.  Stabilizing high pressure over the area diminished the usual moisture laden trade wind pattern.  Only a trace of rain fell in Kahului which usually receives 1.23 inches.  0.42 inches fell in Waimea where the normal for the month is 3.3 inches.  Total rainfall since July was about one-third of normal.   Sugar, onion, and pineapple crops were reduced on Maui, while lettuce and cabbage suffered on Hawaii.  On Hawaii ranchlands, a 10% decrease in cow births was attributed to the drought.

Warm topped convection associated with an upper trough was anchored over the higher slopes of the South Kohala District.  Heavy rainfall occurred in a 3 to 5 mile radius upslope of Highway 190 between Waikoloa Road and Waimea Town.  Flood waters closed parts of Highway 190 with 3 to 4 feet of water.  The flood waters were then carried by a gulch into Waikoloa Village where flooding occurred in condominiums adjacent to a golf course.  Water and mud up to 3 feet deep damaged the lower units of two condominiums, causing $1 million in damages to 36 units.
